perturbation.original_text: Invoices are due and payable within 30 calendar days from the invoice date or from when the amount is due, whichever is later.
perturbation.changed_text: Invoices are due and payable within 60 calendar days from the invoice date or from when the amount is due, whichever is later.
perturbation.explanation: Extended payment terms can negatively affect small businesses and contradict prompt payment expectations. Modifying the payment timeline to 60 days introduces a potential conflict with prompt payment standards, especially if the reseller is considered a small business under applicable laws. This becomes relevant as Diversinet is based in Ontario, Canada, and regulations may exist requiring certain payment terms for small suppliers.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Late Payment of Accounts Receivable Regulation, R.R.O. 2024, Reg. 9/24
perturbation.law_citation: Late Payment of Accounts Receivable Regulation, R.R.O. 2024, Reg. 9/24, s. 5
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation mandates that, starting October 1, 2024, invoices must be paid within 30 days. Changing the text to allow for 60 day payments contradicts this prompt payment regulation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Ontario Newsroom We're sorry but Ontario Newsroom doesn't work properly without JavaScript enabled. Please enable it to continue.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment terms from 30 to 60 days, which directly contradicts the Law Explanation stating invoices must be paid within 30 days starting October 1, 2024. The perturbation explanation points out potential conflict with prompt payment standards, making the extended payment terms illegal if the reseller is considered a small business. The scraped law snippets are unhelpful, but the law explanation provides enough to determine a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term and for seven (7) years after the expiration or termination of this Agreement, or such longer period as required by applicable law, Reseller agrees to maintain complete books, records and accounts relevant to the computation of and accounting for the amounts payable under this Agreement. Reseller agrees to allow Diversinet or its agents and representatives the right to examine and audit such books, records and accounts during Reseller's normal business hours for no more than once per calendar quarter upon reasonable notice.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term and for one (1) year after the expiration or termination of this Agreement, or such longer period as required by applicable law, Reseller agrees to maintain complete books, records and accounts relevant to the computation of and accounting for the amounts payable under this Agreement. Reseller agrees to allow Diversinet or its agents and representatives the right to examine and audit such books, records and accounts during Reseller's normal business hours for no more than once per calendar quarter upon reasonable notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record retention period to one year may conflict with tax laws and regulations that mandate longer retention periods for financial records, especially given the Canadian context of this agreement, and the potential sales to Canadian governmental entities.  For instance, the Income Tax Act (Canada) requires retention for a specified period.
perturbation.law_citation: Income Tax Act, RSC 1985, c. 1 (5th Supp), s 230(4)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 230(4) of the Income Tax Act (Canada) requires taxpayers to keep records and supporting documents for a period longer than one year. The exact length depends on the type of record, but is generally six years from the end of the tax year to which they relate.  Reducing the contractual requirement to one year contradicts this statutory obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: nd that relates to that taxation year, together with every account and voucher necessary to verify the information contained therein, until the expiration of six years from the day the return for that taxation year is filed. Marginal note: Exception where objection or appeal (6) Â Where a person required by this section to keep records and books of account serves a notice of objection or where that person is a party to an appeal to the Tax Court of Canada under this Act, that person shall retain every record, book of account, account and voucher necessary for dealing with the objection or appeal until, in the case of the serving of a notice of objection, the time provided by section 169 to appeal has elapsed or, in the case of an appeal, until the appeal is disposed of and any further appeal in respect thereof is disposed of or the time for filing any such further appeal has expired. Marginal note: Exception where demand by Minister (7) Â Where the Minister is of the opinion that it is necessary for the administration of this Act, the Minister may, by registered letter or by a demand served personally, require any person required by this section to keep records and books of account to retain those records and books of account, together with every account and voucher necessary to verify the information contained therein, for such period as is specified in the letter or demand. Marginal note: Permission for earlier disposal (8) Â A person required by this section to keep records and books of account may dispose of the records and books of account referred to in this section, together with every account and voucher necessary to verify the information contained therein, before the expiration of the period in respect of which those records and books of account are required to be kept if written permission for their disposal is given by the Minister. [NOTE: Application provisions are not included in the consolidated text see relevant amending Acts and regulations.] R.S., 1985, c. 1 (5th Supp.), s. 230 1994, c. 21, s. 105 1998, c. 19, s. 227 2011, perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the record retention period from seven years to one year, while Section 230(4) of the Income Tax Act (Canada), as described in the Law Explanation, generally requires taxpayers to keep records and supporting documents for six years from the end of the tax year. Scraped Law Snippet 1 reinforces this, stating that records must be kept until the expiration of six years from the day the return for that taxation year is filed. The contract's one-year retention period directly contradicts this statutory requirement, making the contract non-compliant and unenforceable to the extent it conflicts with the Income Tax Act.
